review_summary: This unit supports procedural questions about dot-product scoring, softmax normalization, random initialization, and the two-vector word2vec parameterization.

question_bank.question: Why does the word2vec probability model exponentiate dot-product scores before normalizing?
choices: Exponentiation makes the scores positive so they can be normalized into probabilities.
Exponentiation makes every vocabulary item receive the same score.
Exponentiation removes the need for a vocabulary-wide denominator.
Exponentiation turns dense vectors into exact dictionary definitions.
question_bank.explanation: Dot products are arbitrary real-valued scores. Exponentiating makes them positive, and dividing by the sum over vocabulary turns them into a probability distribution.

question_bank.question: What role does softmax play in the word2vec context prediction model?
choices: It turns unbounded scores for possible context words into a probability distribution that sums to one.
It selects one hard maximum word and assigns zero probability to all others.
It replaces gradient descent with a fixed lookup table.
It guarantees that all word vectors remain one-hot.
question_bank.explanation: Softmax converts a list of real-valued scores into normalized probabilities. This lets the model assign likelihoods to possible outside words.

question_bank.question: How does training begin in the word2vec setup?
choices: The word vectors start randomly and are adjusted to increase probabilities of observed contexts.
The vectors are copied directly from WordNet definitions.
The model starts with perfect contextual embeddings for every token.
The vectors are fixed one-hot encodings and never updated.
question_bank.explanation: The parameters are learned rather than supplied by hand. Starting from random vectors, optimization changes them so observed word-context pairs become more likely.

question_bank.question: Why does this word2vec formulation use two vectors per vocabulary item?
choices: A word has one vector when it is the center word and another when it is an outside word.
One vector stores spelling and the other stores capitalization.
One vector is always one-hot and the other is always a WordNet entry.
The two vectors are used only to make plotting in 2D easier.
question_bank.explanation: The same word can play two different roles in the prediction model. Separate center and outside vectors make the probability parameterization cleaner.
